title: Insolvency Act 1986
jurisdiction: England and Wales (with parallel provisions for Scotland)
chapters: 444 sections + 14 Schedules in 19 Parts. Employment-relevant provisions centred on: Part XII (ss.386-387 + Schedule 6 — preferential debts including employee claims), Part I (ss.1-7 — Company Voluntary Arrangements), Part II (ss.8-27 + Schedule B1 — Administration), Part IV (ss.73-160 — Winding Up/Liquidation). Employee claims for unpaid debts are governed by ERA 1996 Part XII (ss.182-190) which operates alongside the IA 1986 creditor priority framework. The Regulations must be read together with TUPE 2006 (protection of employees on business transfer from an insolvent employer) and the Pensions Act 2004 (Pension Protection Fund).

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — Conservative Government, based on the recommendations of the Cork Report (Report of the Review Committee on Insolvency Law and Practice, Cmnd 8558, 1982)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: The Act consolidated and reformed UK insolvency law. It has been substantially amended since enactment, particularly by the Enterprise Act 2002 (which reformed administration and abolished Crown preference), the Corporate Insolvency and Governance Act 2020 (which introduced moratoriums and restructuring plans), and the Insolvency (England and Wales) Rules 2016 (which replaced the 1986 Rules).

significantAmendments.effect: Abolished Crown preference (HMRC's preferential creditor status was removed, increasing funds available for employee preferential claims and unsecured creditors). Reformed administration — introduced a streamlined 'administration order' process. Created a ring-fenced fund (the 'prescribed part') for unsecured creditors from floating charge realisations.

RESTORED HMRC as a secondary preferential creditor for certain taxes (VAT, PAYE, employee NICs, CIS deductions) from 1 December 2020. This REDUCES the funds available for employees and unsecured creditors because HMRC now takes priority over floating charge creditors again for these amounts.

Introduced new rescue mechanisms: free-standing moratorium (giving companies breathing space), restructuring plan (cross-class cram down), and prohibition on supplier termination clauses in contracts with insolvent companies. The moratorium protects employees because the company continues to trade and pay wages during the moratorium period.

precedingDocuments.title: Cork Report (Cmnd 8558, 1982)
precedingDocuments.significance: The foundational review that recommended the modern insolvency framework. The Cork Committee emphasised the importance of employee protection in insolvency, leading to the preferential creditor status for employee claims.

precedingDocuments.title: ERA 1996 Part XII (ss.182-190) — Employee's Rights on Insolvency of Employer
precedingDocuments.significance: The companion legislation that gives employees the right to claim SPECIFIC debts from the NATIONAL INSURANCE FUND when their employer becomes insolvent. ERA Part XII and IA 1986 Schedule 6 are the two parallel routes by which employees can recover money owed to them.

leadingCases.name: Re Nortel GmbH
leadingCases.principle: The priority waterfall in insolvency was confirmed: (1) fixed charge creditors, (2) expenses of insolvency proceedings, (3) preferential creditors (including employees), (4) floating charge creditors, (5) unsecured creditors, (6) statutory interest, (7) shareholders. Employee preferential claims rank at position 3.
leadingCases.heldText: Lord Neuberger set out the order of priority for payment out of the company's assets in a liquidation or administration.
leadingCases.significance: The definitive statement of the creditor priority waterfall. Employees as preferential creditors rank ABOVE floating charge creditors and unsecured creditors — but BELOW fixed charge creditors and the costs of the insolvency process itself.

parts.title: Part 1 — Employee Claims in Insolvency: The Two Routes

sections.num: IA 1986 Part XII + Schedule 6 | ERA 1996 Part XII (ss.182-190)
sections.title: How employees recover money owed by an insolvent employer — preferential creditor status and the National Insurance Fund
sections.summary: When an employer becomes insolvent, employees who are owed money have TWO routes to recover payment: (1) as PREFERENTIAL CREDITORS under the IA 1986 Schedule 6 — claiming from the insolvent estate with priority over floating charge creditors and unsecured creditors, and (2) via the NATIONAL INSURANCE FUND under ERA 1996 ss.182-190 — claiming certain statutory debts from the Government, administered by the Redundancy Payments Service (RPS). The two routes are complementary: the NIF covers specific statutory entitlements (redundancy pay, arrears of pay, holiday pay, notice pay); preferential creditor status covers unpaid wages and holiday pay from the insolvent estate. Where the NIF pays an employee's debt, the Secretary of State is subrogated to the employee's claim and takes the employee's place as a preferential or ordinary creditor.
sections.text:
ROUTE 1 — PREFERENTIAL CREDITOR STATUS (IA 1986 s.175 + Schedule 6):

Employees are treated as PREFERENTIAL CREDITORS for:
(a) Unpaid wages for the 4 months preceding the insolvency order, subject to a cap of £800 per employee.
(b) Accrued holiday pay — the TOTAL amount, with NO cap.
(c) Pension contributions owed by the employer.

Priority position: preferential creditors rank at position 3 in the waterfall:
(1) Fixed charge creditors
(2) Expenses of the insolvency process
(3) PREFERENTIAL CREDITORS (employees + HMRC secondary preference from Dec 2020)
(4) Prescribed part fund (for unsecured creditors — max £800,000)
(5) Floating charge creditors
(6) Unsecured creditors
(7) Statutory interest
(8) Shareholders

ROUTE 2 — NATIONAL INSURANCE FUND (ERA 1996 ss.182-190):

Employees can claim the following from the NIF via the Redundancy Payments Service:
(a) Statutory redundancy pay — calculated by age, service (max 20 years), and weekly pay (capped at £643 per week from 6 April 2024).
(b) Arrears of pay — up to 8 weeks, capped at £643 per week.
(c) Holiday pay — up to 6 weeks, capped at £643 per week.
(d) Statutory notice pay — minimum notice period (1 week per year of service, max 12 weeks), capped at £643 per week.
(e) Unpaid basic award of compensation for unfair dismissal.
(f) Any unpaid protective award (for failure to consult on collective redundancies).

The NIF will ONLY pay if the employer has become FORMALLY INSOLVENT (administration, liquidation, CVA, bankruptcy). The employee must claim via the Government's online portal or the Redundancy Payments Service.

IMPORTANT: Statutory redundancy pay can be claimed from the NIF even if the employer is NOT formally insolvent — the employee can apply if the employer simply refuses to pay (ERA 1996 s.170). But other NIF payments require formal insolvency.

STATUTORY SICK PAY AND MATERNITY PAY:
— SSP and SMP owed at the time of insolvency may be covered by HMRC under separate regulations (Statutory Sick Pay (General) Regulations 1982 Reg.9A, Statutory Maternity Pay (General) Regulations 1986 Reg.7).

PENSION CONTRIBUTIONS:
— Unpaid employer pension contributions may be claimed from the NIF.
— For defined benefit schemes, the Pension Protection Fund (PPF) under the Pensions Act 2004 may step in to protect pension benefits if the scheme is underfunded.

INFORMAL INSOLVENCY — WHEN THE EMPLOYER JUST DISAPPEARS:

Not all employer failures involve formal insolvency proceedings. Sometimes the employer simply stops trading: the premises close, the directors become unreachable, and employees are left without pay, notice, or explanation. This is informal insolvency — and the employee's position is significantly worse.

(1) NIF claims for arrears of pay, holiday pay, and notice pay require FORMAL insolvency. If no formal insolvency process has been initiated, the employee cannot access the NIF for these debts.
(2) HOWEVER, statutory redundancy pay can be claimed from the NIF even without formal insolvency — the employee applies directly to the Redundancy Payments Service, which will investigate the employer's status.
(3) The employee (or creditor) can petition the court to wind up the company compulsorily (IA 1986 s.124). This triggers formal insolvency and unlocks NIF access.
(4) HMRC may also petition for compulsory winding up where the employer owes unpaid taxes.
(5) Practical advice for employees: contact the Insolvency Service, contact ACAS, consider an employment tribunal claim for unfair dismissal and unpaid wages.

PHOENIX COMPANIES:

A 'phoenix company' arises when directors allow a company to become insolvent, then immediately (or shortly after) incorporate a new company to continue the same business — often with the same name, same premises, same customers, but without the old company's debts and liabilities.

(1) Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986 (CDDA): directors who cause a company to trade while insolvent, or who allow company assets to be stripped before insolvency, may be DISQUALIFIED from acting as directors for up to 15 years.
(2) IA 1986 s.216-217: a person who was a director of a company that went into insolvent liquidation is prohibited from being a director of (or being involved in the management of) a company with the same or a similar name for 5 years — the 'phoenix' restriction. Breach is a criminal offence.
(3) IA 1986 s.214: wrongful trading — if a director knew or ought to have concluded that there was no reasonable prospect of the company avoiding insolvent liquidation and continued to trade, the court may order the director to contribute personally to the company's assets.
(4) For employees: phoenix company activity is a red flag. The Insolvency Service investigates and may pursue director disqualification. Employees should report suspected phoenix activity to the Insolvency Service.

STEP-BY-STEP NIF CLAIM PROCESS:

(1) The insolvency practitioner (administrator, liquidator) will normally write to all employees providing a claim form (RP1) and an explanation of NIF entitlements.
(2) If no insolvency practitioner is appointed (informal insolvency), the employee should apply directly via GOV.UK: www.gov.uk/claim-redundancy.
(3) Required documentation: P45 or latest payslip, contract of employment, details of service dates, weekly pay, holiday entitlement, notice period.
(4) Processing time: typically 3-6 weeks from receipt of a complete application. Complex cases may take longer.
(5) If the claim is disputed (e.g., the Redundancy Payments Service questions the employment status or dates of service), the employee may need to provide additional evidence or refer the matter to an employment tribunal.
(6) Payments are made directly to the employee by BACS transfer.
sections.keyPrinciple: The employee is NOT left without remedy when their employer becomes insolvent. The NIF provides a Government-backed safety net for specific debts, and preferential creditor status gives employees priority over most other creditors in the distribution of the insolvent estate. However, both routes have caps and limits — the employee may not recover the FULL amount owed. The HR manager (or insolvency practitioner) has a responsibility to inform affected employees of their NIF claim rights and to provide the documentation needed to make claims.
refs: IA 1986 s.175 (distribution of property — preferential debts have priority)
IA 1986 Schedule 6 (list of preferential debts — employees)
ERA 1996 s.166-170 (redundancy pay claims — including from NIF where employer refuses to pay)
Finance Act 2020 s.98 (HMRC secondary preferential status from Dec 2020)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Claim money owed by a bankrupt employer
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance for employees on how to claim from the NIF when their employer becomes insolvent.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Guide for employees when a company enters insolvency
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The Insolvency Service provides guidance and administers the Redundancy Payments Service.

commonErrors: Not knowing the difference between the two routes — the NIF (ERA 1996) and preferential creditor status (IA 1986) are SEPARATE mechanisms. An employee may claim through both, but for different debts

Assuming all debts are fully recoverable — both routes have CAPS. The NIF caps weekly pay at £643 (from April 2024). Preferential creditor status caps unpaid wages at £800 for the 4-month pre-insolvency period. High-earning employees will not recover their full entitlements

Not applying to the NIF promptly — there is no statutory deadline but the Redundancy Payments Service recommends claiming as soon as possible after the employer becomes insolvent. Delay can complicate evidence-gathering

Failing to distinguish between statutory redundancy pay (which can be claimed from the NIF even without formal insolvency) and other NIF payments (which require formal insolvency) — this distinction matters when an employer simply stops trading without entering a formal insolvency process

Not informing employees of their NIF rights — the insolvency practitioner has a duty to notify employees, but in practice HR managers (if still in post) play a critical role in helping employees understand and access their entitlements

Not knowing what to do when the employer simply disappears without formal insolvency — the employee should: (a) apply for statutory redundancy pay via GOV.UK (available without formal insolvency), (b) contact the Insolvency Service for guidance, (c) consider petitioning for compulsory winding up to trigger NIF access for other debts, (d) bring an employment tribunal claim for unfair dismissal and unpaid wages

Not being aware of phoenix company restrictions — if directors of an insolvent company immediately set up a new company doing the same business, this may breach IA 1986 s.216 (same/similar name restriction) and CDDA 1986 (director disqualification). Employees should report suspected phoenix activity to the Insolvency Service

parts.title: Part 2 — Types of Insolvency and Their Employment Consequences

sections.title: Administration, liquidation, CVA, moratorium — what each means for employees
sections.summary: The type of insolvency process determines what happens to employees. In ADMINISTRATION (IA 1986 Schedule B1), the purpose is to rescue the company or achieve a better result for creditors than immediate liquidation. The administrator may continue to trade the business, retain employees, and sell the business as a going concern — TUPE applies to such sales. In LIQUIDATION (compulsory winding up or creditors' voluntary liquidation), the purpose is to realise assets and distribute proceeds to creditors. Contracts of employment are typically TERMINATED — employees are dismissed by reason of redundancy. TUPE does NOT apply to sales of assets out of liquidation (only to sales as a going concern from administration). In a COMPANY VOLUNTARY ARRANGEMENT (CVA), the company continues to trade under a composition with creditors — employees' positions are usually preserved unless the CVA plan includes redundancies. The CIGA 2020 MORATORIUM gives a company breathing space — employees continue to be employed and paid during the moratorium.
sections.text:
ADMINISTRATION (IA 1986 Schedule B1):
(1) Purpose: rescue the company as a going concern, or achieve a better result for creditors than winding up.
(2) Employment impact: the administrator takes control. Employees are NOT automatically dismissed — their contracts continue.
(3) The administrator may retain employees, make redundancies, or sell the business.
(4) If the business is sold as a going concern: TUPE 2006 APPLIES — employees transfer to the buyer with their existing terms and conditions.
(5) 'Adopted contracts' — if the administrator continues to employ workers for more than 14 days, those contracts are treated as 'adopted' and the administrator's fees and employees' wages during the period of adoption rank as an expense of the administration (ahead of preferential creditors).
(6) Pre-pack administration: a pre-arranged sale of the business immediately or shortly after the appointment of an administrator. TUPE applies. Employees transfer.

LIQUIDATION (IA 1986 Part IV):
(1) Purpose: wind up the company, realise all assets, distribute proceeds.
(2) Employment impact: contracts of employment are TERMINATED. Employees are dismissed by reason of redundancy.
(3) TUPE does NOT apply to asset sales in liquidation — only to going concern sales.
(4) Employees become creditors — preferential creditors for wages/holiday pay, unsecured for the remainder.
(5) NIF claims become available immediately upon formal insolvency.

COMPANY VOLUNTARY ARRANGEMENT (CVA) (IA 1986 Part I):
(1) Purpose: a binding agreement between the company and its creditors to restructure debts.
(2) Employment impact: the company continues to trade. Employees' positions are usually preserved unless the CVA plan includes cost reduction measures (which may include redundancies).
(3) Employee debts are usually addressed through the CVA — arrears of wages may be paid in full or compromised.

MORATORIUM (CIGA 2020):
(1) Purpose: give the company breathing space from creditor action while a rescue plan is developed.
(2) Employment impact: employees continue to be employed and paid. The moratorium itself does not affect contracts of employment.
(3) Duration: initially 20 business days, extendable.

CRITICAL DISTINCTION — TUPE IN INSOLVENCY:
— TUPE applies to sales from ADMINISTRATION (going concern).
— TUPE does NOT apply to sales from LIQUIDATION.
— In administration, TUPE Reg.8(7) allows the administrator to agree variations to employment terms that would otherwise be void under TUPE, provided the variations are designed to safeguard employment.
— These 'permitted variations' are significant — they allow the administrator to reduce pay, change terms, or restructure the workforce to make the business viable for sale.
sections.keyPrinciple: The type of insolvency process determines whether employees keep their jobs (administration/CVA/moratorium) or lose them (liquidation). For the HR manager, the critical question when an employer enters insolvency is: IS THIS A RESCUE PROCESS OR A WIND-DOWN? In a rescue, TUPE protects employees on transfer. In a wind-down, employees are dismissed and must claim from the NIF and as preferential creditors.
refs: TUPE 2006 Reg.8(6) (TUPE does not apply in liquidation)
commonErrors: Assuming TUPE applies to ALL insolvency sales — TUPE applies to going concern sales from ADMINISTRATION but NOT to asset sales from LIQUIDATION. This distinction determines whether employees transfer with their terms or are dismissed

Not understanding 'adopted contracts' in administration — if the administrator keeps employees for more than 14 days, those employees' wages rank as an EXPENSE of the administration, ahead of preferential creditors. Administrators are acutely aware of this and may make early redundancy decisions to avoid adoption

Not advising employees of their TUPE rights when a sale from administration is proposed — employees have the right to be informed and consulted about the transfer under TUPE Reg.13, even in insolvency

Assuming a CVA preserves all jobs — a CVA is a debt restructuring plan that may include cost reduction measures, which can include redundancies. Employees should not assume their positions are safe simply because the company enters a CVA

Not claiming from the NIF immediately upon formal insolvency — the Redundancy Payments Service can take several weeks to process claims. Early application reduces financial hardship for affected employees

parts.title: Part 3 — Impact Assessment: What to Do When You Suddenly Lose Your Job to Insolvency

sections.num: Cross-reference: ERA 1996 + benefits legislation + practical signposting
sections.title: The employee's crisis response guide — priority actions in the first 48 hours, first week, and first month
sections.summary: When an employer enters insolvency and employees lose their jobs, the immediate experience is shock. Unlike a normal redundancy — where there is consultation, notice, and time to prepare — an insolvency dismissal can happen overnight. The employee may arrive at work to find the doors locked. Eileen must be able to guide the employee through the immediate crisis with clarity, compassion, and practical signposting. This section provides the priority actions framework: what to do FIRST, what can wait, and where to find help. It is designed to be the basis for a downloadable signposting document that Eileen can generate for any employee affected by employer insolvency.
sections.text:
THE FIRST 48 HOURS — IMMEDIATE PRIORITIES:

(1) SECURE YOUR FINANCIAL POSITION:
(a) Apply for Universal Credit IMMEDIATELY via GOV.UK or telephone (0800 328 5644). UC can take 5 weeks for the first payment — the earlier you apply, the earlier the clock starts. You can request an advance payment if you are in immediate financial difficulty.
(b) If you have a mortgage, contact your lender within the first week. Many lenders offer a 'payment holiday' or forbearance for employees made redundant. Do NOT simply stop paying — this will damage your credit record.
(c) If you rent, contact your landlord to explain the situation. If you receive Housing Benefit or the housing element of UC, your housing costs may be partially covered.
(d) Contact your bank if you have overdraft facilities or credit commitments. Many banks have financial difficulty teams who can offer temporary relief.
(e) If you have dependent children, check eligibility for Child Benefit (if not already claimed) and Free School Meals.

(2) GATHER YOUR DOCUMENTS:
(a) Your contract of employment (or written statement of terms).
(b) Your most recent payslips (at least 12 weeks — needed for NIF calculations).
(c) Your P45 or latest P60.
(d) Any letter from the insolvency practitioner (administrator, liquidator).
(e) Records of any accrued but untaken holiday.
(f) Records of your length of continuous service (start date).
(g) Any correspondence about redundancy, consultation, or changes to your employment.
(h) If you are pursuing or considering an employment tribunal claim: ALL documents relating to the claim, including ET1 form, correspondence, witness statements, and evidence.

(3) CONTACT THE INSOLVENCY PRACTITIONER:
(a) The administrator or liquidator will usually write to all employees with information about their rights.
(b) If you have not received a letter, contact the insolvency practitioner directly. Their details will be published in the London Gazette and on Companies House.
(c) Ask the insolvency practitioner for: confirmation of your dismissal date, a RP1 form (for redundancy pay claims), and details of how to claim from the National Insurance Fund.

THE FIRST WEEK — CLAIM FROM THE NATIONAL INSURANCE FUND:

(4) APPLY TO THE REDUNDANCY PAYMENTS SERVICE (RPS):
(a) Apply online at GOV.UK: www.gov.uk/claim-redundancy (search 'claim money owed by employer who is insolvent').
(b) You can claim: statutory redundancy pay, arrears of pay (up to 8 weeks), holiday pay (up to 6 weeks), statutory notice pay.
(c) All payments are capped at £643 per week (from April 2024). If your weekly pay exceeded this, you will not recover the full amount.
(d) The RPS aims to process claims within 3-6 weeks of receiving a complete application.
(e) If you are unsure whether the insolvency process has been formally started, check the London Gazette or Companies House for the insolvency notice.

(5) REGISTER FOR NEW-STYLE JOBSEEKER'S ALLOWANCE (if applicable):
(a) If you have sufficient National Insurance contributions, you may be eligible for New-Style JSA (contribution-based) in addition to UC.
(b) Apply via GOV.UK or Jobcentre Plus.
(c) New-Style JSA can be paid for up to 6 months.

THE FIRST MONTH — LONGER-TERM PLANNING:

(6) PENSION RIGHTS:
(a) If you were in the employer's workplace pension scheme, your accrued pension benefits are PROTECTED — they do not disappear because the employer is insolvent.
(b) For defined contribution schemes: your fund is held by the pension provider, not the employer. It is SAFE.
(c) For defined benefit (final salary) schemes: if the scheme is underfunded, the Pension Protection Fund (PPF) may step in to protect your benefits (at approximately 90% of the full amount for those below normal pension age).
(d) Contact the pension scheme trustees or the PPF for information.

(7) SEEK SPECIALIST ADVICE:
(a) Citizens Advice (0800 144 8848) — free advice on benefits, debt, and employment rights.
(b) ACAS (0300 123 1100) — advice on employment rights, redundancy, and tribunal claims.
(c) If you are considering or already pursuing an employment tribunal claim against the insolvent employer: seek legal advice from a specialist employment solicitor. Many offer a free initial consultation.
(d) If you have a trade union: contact your union representative immediately. The union can provide legal assistance and representation.

(8) EMOTIONAL AND MENTAL HEALTH SUPPORT:
(a) Losing your job suddenly is a traumatic experience. It is normal to feel shock, anger, anxiety, and grief.
(b) Talk to family, friends, or a trusted person.
(c) If you are struggling, contact: Samaritans (116 123, free, 24/7), MIND (0300 123 3393), or your GP for a referral to NHS talking therapies.
(d) Do not make major life decisions in the first week if you can avoid it. Give yourself time to process.
sections.keyPrinciple: Eileen's role when someone loses their job to insolvency is NOT to provide legal advice on their specific case. It IS to ensure they know: (a) they have RIGHTS and those rights have not disappeared because the employer is insolvent, (b) the PRIORITY ACTIONS they should take in the first 48 hours, first week, and first month, (c) WHERE to find specialist help, and (d) that they are not alone. This content is designed to be the basis for a downloadable signposting document — a single page that an affected employee can take away and work through.
refs: GOV.UK: Claim money owed by employer who is insolvent
commonErrors: Not applying for Universal Credit immediately — UC takes up to 5 weeks for the first payment. Every day of delay extends the gap. Apply on the SAME DAY you are told your job has gone

Not knowing that pension benefits are PROTECTED — many employees panic that their pension has been lost. Defined contribution pots are held by the provider, not the employer. Defined benefit schemes are protected by the PPF

Waiting for the insolvency practitioner to contact you — the IP will usually write to employees, but in chaotic insolvencies this may be delayed. The employee should proactively seek out the IP's contact details (London Gazette, Companies House) and apply to the RPS independently

Not gathering documents before access to the workplace is lost — once an employer enters liquidation, access to the premises may be restricted. The employee should gather employment documents, payslips, and personal belongings as early as possible
Not seeking mental health support — sudden job loss through insolvency is a grief event. The employee's wellbeing must be addressed alongside their financial and legal position

parts.title: Part 4 — Employment Tribunal Claims Against an Insolvent Employer

sections.title: What happens when your employer enters insolvency during or before your tribunal claim
sections.summary: One of the most distressing scenarios for an employee: you are pursuing an employment tribunal claim against your employer — for unfair dismissal, discrimination, unpaid wages, or a protective award — and the employer enters insolvency. Does your claim survive? Can you still get compensation? The answer is nuanced: the claim SURVIVES (the company remains a legal entity even in liquidation), but enforcing any AWARD may be impossible if the company has no assets. However, certain awards can be claimed from the National Insurance Fund, and the employee may need the tribunal judgment to prove the debt. Eileen must be able to explain these options clearly.
sections.text:
SCENARIO: YOUR EMPLOYER ENTERS INSOLVENCY DURING YOUR ET CLAIM.

STEP 1 — DOES THE CLAIM SURVIVE?
(a) YES. A company in administration or liquidation remains a legal entity. The tribunal claim can continue.
(b) In ADMINISTRATION: the administrator may defend the claim if it affects the business's value.
(c) In LIQUIDATION: the liquidator may choose not to defend the claim (because there is little point in spending money on legal fees if the company has no assets).
(d) If the employer (respondent) does not defend, the tribunal may issue a DEFAULT JUDGMENT in the employee's favour.

STEP 2 — CAN YOU ENFORCE THE AWARD?
(a) Any MONETARY AWARD (compensation, unpaid wages, redundancy pay, protective award) becomes a DEBT owed by the insolvent company.
(b) If the company is in LIQUIDATION: the debt ranks as an unsecured creditor claim (unless it falls within the preferential creditor categories). In practice, unsecured creditors in liquidation often receive little or nothing.
(c) HOWEVER: certain elements of the tribunal award CAN be claimed from the National Insurance Fund:
— Basic award of compensation for unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.182(1)(d)).
— Protective award (for failure to consult on collective redundancies) (ERA 1996 s.182(1)(e)).
— Arrears of pay, holiday pay, notice pay (up to the NIF caps).
(d) IMPORTANT: the NIF will usually require a TRIBUNAL JUDGMENT or other formal determination of the debt before paying. This means the employee should CONTINUE the tribunal claim to obtain a judgment, even if the employer is not defending — the judgment is the evidence needed for the NIF claim.

STEP 3 — THE COMPENSATORY AWARD AND DISCRIMINATION AWARDS:
(a) The COMPENSATORY award for unfair dismissal (above the basic award) is NOT covered by the NIF. It ranks as an unsecured debt in the insolvency.
(b) DISCRIMINATION awards (injury to feelings, aggravated damages) are NOT covered by the NIF. They rank as unsecured debts.
(c) In practice, these awards are often unrecoverable against an insolvent employer unless the company's assets are sufficient to reach unsecured creditors (which is rare).
(d) EXCEPTION: if the discrimination was by an INDIVIDUAL (a named manager), the employee may pursue the claim against the individual respondent personally — the individual's personal liability survives the company's insolvency.

STEP 4 — PRACTICAL STEPS FOR THE EMPLOYEE:
(a) CONTINUE the tribunal claim — you need the judgment to prove the debt for NIF purposes.
(b) APPLY to the NIF for any elements that are covered (basic award, protective award, arrears, holiday pay, notice pay).
(c) REGISTER as a creditor with the liquidator or administrator — submit a proof of debt for any amounts not covered by the NIF.
(d) If the claim includes DISCRIMINATION against a named individual: consider whether to pursue the individual personally.
(e) SEEK LEGAL ADVICE — the interaction between tribunal claims and insolvency is complex. Many employment solicitors offer free initial consultations. Your trade union (if applicable) may provide legal assistance.
(f) CHECK whether the employer had EMPLOYER'S LIABILITY INSURANCE or PROFESSIONAL INDEMNITY INSURANCE — in some cases, the insurer may be liable to meet the award even though the employer is insolvent.

STEP 5 — WHAT IF INSOLVENCY MAKES THE CLAIM POINTLESS?
(a) Sometimes the employee concludes that pursuing the claim is not worth the time and emotional energy if no meaningful compensation will be recovered.
(b) This is a PERSONAL DECISION — there is no obligation to continue.
(c) However, the employee should consider: (i) whether any NIF-covered elements make continuing worthwhile, (ii) whether a named individual can be pursued personally, and (iii) whether the principle of the claim matters to the employee regardless of financial recovery.
(d) Eileen should present the options neutrally and support the employee's informed decision — not push them towards or away from continuing.
sections.keyPrinciple: An ongoing tribunal claim against an insolvent employer is not automatically pointless. Certain elements (basic award, protective award, arrears, holiday pay, notice pay) can be claimed from the NIF — but the NIF usually needs a tribunal judgment to process the claim. The employee should continue the claim to obtain the judgment, then use it to claim from the NIF. Discrimination awards and compensatory awards above the NIF caps are usually unrecoverable unless a named individual can be pursued. Eileen must explain these realities with honesty and sensitivity — helping the employee make an informed decision about whether to continue.
refs: ERA 1996 s.166-170 (redundancy pay claims from NIF)
Employment Tribunals Rules of Procedure 2013 (default judgments)
EqA 2010 s.110 (individual liability for discrimination — survives company insolvency)
Redundancy Payments Service (RPS) — online claims portal
commonErrors: Abandoning the tribunal claim because the employer is insolvent — the employee should usually CONTINUE to obtain a judgment, because the judgment is needed to claim NIF-covered elements

Not knowing which parts of a tribunal award are covered by the NIF — basic award YES, protective award YES, arrears/holiday/notice YES (capped), compensatory award NO, discrimination award NO. The employee needs to understand this distinction

Forgetting that individual respondents named in a discrimination claim remain personally liable — if the employer is insolvent but a named manager was found to have discriminated, the employee can pursue the individual for the injury to feelings and other discrimination awards

Not registering as a creditor with the liquidator — even for amounts not covered by the NIF, the employee should submit a proof of debt. There is a small chance the liquidation will produce sufficient assets to pay unsecured creditors
Not checking employer's liability insurance — in some cases, the insurer may be liable to satisfy the tribunal award even though the employer company is insolvent

pendingAmendments.status: The weekly cap on NIF payments is reviewed annually. Current cap: £643 per week (from April 2024). Monitor for the April 2025/2026 update.

Since December 2020, HMRC has secondary preferential status for certain taxes. This reduces the pool available for employee preferential claims and unsecured creditors. Monitor for data on the practical impact.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — HMRC preference reduces employee recovery from insolvent estates
